channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Memoirium Preview - King's Field Trip
item.content:encoded:
Before there was Dark Souls, there was Demon's Souls, but before even that, there was King's Field. FromSoftware's seminal (if creaky by modern standards) dungeon-crawling adventure served not only as an inspiration for the aforementioned games, but also seemingly for today's subject, Memoirium.

Taking place in a dreamlike landscape filled with surreal architecture and plenty of floating furniture, Memoirium melds the third-person exploration and combat of the Soulslike genre with the low-poly look and eerie atmosphere of King's Field (and, to a lesser extent, Shadow Tower).

I got to sit down with the demo ahead of its release as part of the latest Steam Next Fest extravaganza, and I came away tentatively excited to check out the full game, although there are a few issues that developer GoldenGratus will need to iron out prior to this one's debut in the big leagues.

Memoirium Convincingly Evokes King's Field

Memoirium bills itself as a Soulslike, but it's far better at evoking the woozy, dreamlike PlayStation-era vibes of King's Field than the ruined grandiosity of FromSoftware's later franchise.

I explored two key locations during my time with the demo: the tutorial area, which consists of a forest rendered in vivid reds and purples, and a more subdued academy with a panopticon-style light constantly scanning for undesirable elements (i.e. the player).

Both were atmospheric and compelling, managing to evoke the hazy, topsy-turvy logic of a dream without ever feeling arbitrary or convoluted.

That's also thanks in large part to the level design, which gives plenty of opportunities to wander off the beaten path to take on extra enemies and hunt down loot, although I'd have liked to see a few more surprises in the largely flat and linear school area.

Naturally, the demo doesn't give much of a sense of what Memoirium's story will look like; the Soulslike genre is well-known for its elliptical and ambiguous storytelling anyway, and this is a small slice of the finished product.

Nevertheless, I came away from Memoirium intrigued by its setting and excited to explore more of its winding corridors and nocturnal woods.

Admittedly, it can sometimes feel like it's lacking an identity of its own, particularly in the dialogue stakes; Memoirium falls back rather often on the fragmentary dialogue and lofty Proper Noun Usage that can sometimes get a touch tedious in the genre's lesser examples.

Still, the wobbly polygon aesthetic and environmental creativity on display made me want to delve deeper into Memoirium's world and uncover its secrets.

Combat Feels Wonky in Memoirium

It's a good thing, too, because Memoirium's combat is going to need some work before it's ready to come out of the oven.

Since this is a Soulslike, you know what to expect if you've played any previous games in the genre; expect lock-on-based fights that incorporate stamina management, watching for tells, and punishing mistakes.

Memoirium's combat is undone by one fatal mistake on this front. Its weapons simply don't feel like much fun to use.

In essentially every combat encounter across my time with Memoirium's demo, it was virtually impossible to land more than one or two hits on any enemy before they would inexplicably dance out of range or before the weapon I was using would simply stop hitting them.

This made regular enemy fights last much longer than they needed to and turned the two bosses I battled in the demo into frustrating, repetitive slogs, defined mainly by my patience running out rather than being pushed to the limits of my skill.

Landing blows in Memoirium doesn't feel particularly great, either. There's a weightless floatiness to the combat that will need to be addressed if GoldenGratus wants to compete in this insanely crowded genre.

I'm also a little worried about enemy variety, which has proven the killing blow for many other Soulslikes. I wish I'd been wrongfooted by more unusual, non-humanoid enemies in the Memoirium demo; with visuals this deliberately lo-fi, there's no real excuse for not getting creative in this (king's) field.

Memoirium Preview | Final Thoughts

I'm cautiously optimistic about Memoirium. It's got a unique aesthetic and plenty of charm and heart, and it's clearly been built with love for the Soulslike genre.

If it wants to stand out in a busy field, though, it's going to need to spruce up its combat and throw a few more enemy curveballs. Here's hoping the full version can do just that when it arrives in August.

Memoirium was previewed on PC using a code provided by the publisher over the course of around 90 minutes of gameplay. All screenshots were taken during the preview process.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/memoirium-preview-kings-field-trip
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Ravenloft: The Horrors Within Teaches How To Add Horror Instead Of Just What Horror Is
item.content:encoded:
Dungeons & Dragons has returned to the Domains of Dread in Ravenloft: The Horrors Within. Only five years after Van Richten's Guide to Ravenloft, and with plenty of prep time for your yearly Halloween one-shot, how does this new source book and its horror-themed player options stack up?

To start, the book opens with the immutable facts about Ravenloft. What Ravenloft is, what a darklord is, and the rules that they must follow.

They are prisons for the Darklords, who, while they might rule, are twisted by the circumstances of the realm, trapping them in a cycle. This section also goes over how the mists that surround the Domains of Dread also function, including what happens to players who get lost wandering the mists.

This section also does a good job outlining the larger pieces of information that you'll need to be aware of, but also some of the more minute details. Specific rules are outlined for the effects of spells like banishment and how they work differently, how spells like Sending can't send messages outside the mists, and even fun cosmetic changes like creatures summoned by Find Familiar might appear more sinister than they traditionally do.

This serves as a good top-level overview of just what the concept of Ravenloft and the Domains of Dread are. Honestly, there are a lot of times where Dungeons & Dragons approaches a topic like the reader already has a familiarity with the concept. This easing in helps establish the rules and foundation of knowledge, for the player and the DM, before providing additional context to Character Options, and DM information on not just creating stories in Ravenloft, but creating their own Domains of Dread.

Character Options In Ravenloft: The Horrors Within Inform Story

Ravenloft: The Horrors Within offers a variety of character options in the form of Sub-Classes, Species, Backgrounds, Feats, and more. Before getting to the mechanical options, there are a number of questions and scenarios posed to the player to help them better inform the creation of a character that is going to fit into a world of horror better.

Questions posed might be about what dark secrets in their past could have led to their present, what might your character find unsettling or spooky, and to understand that this isn't a high-fantasy adventure where the hero always wears a smile on their face, there can be a lot to learn from the moments your characters falter.

"Knowing what frightens your character provides insight into their past and can motivate their behavior"

This is again one of those moments I feel that the book isn't just presenting mechanical options or a wall of options to the player, but instead the player and DM are getting taught what kinds of things to think about when shaping a game to help build it into a deeper experience.

The Sub-Classes available in Ravenloft: The Horrors Within are the Reanimator Artificer, College of Spirits Bard, Grave Domain Cleric, Phantom Rogue, Shadow Sorcery Sorcerer, and an Undead Patron Warlock.

From our interview with the design team behind Ravenloft: The Horrors Within, we know that if you purchase this book through D&D Beyond, even if you haven't purchased Eberron: Forge of the Artificer, you'll get full access to the base Artificer class

The Reanimator is a fun take on a Dr Frankenstein-like character. Alterations to Spare the Dying let you revive your party member with electricity that sparks from them, dealing an additional 2d4 Lightning damage to those within 10 feet of your target. You also get a Reanimated Companion that you can move in combat on your turn using your Bonus Action.

As you level up your Artificer, you can also add additional benefits to your Reanimated Companion, including letting them do more damage or letting you cast spells through them, or making them large, faster, or 'moist,' which gives them a swim speed and the ability to move through a space as narrow as 1 inch.

Quite frankly, this class is just a bit gross, but for the party 'pet' that will be following them around will prompt for a LOT of great horror moments.

The other fully new subclass for D&D 5.5e is the Hollow Warden Ranger. These Rangers pull their powers from the strange creatures of the "old places of the earth." You can expend a use of Favored Enemy to transform into a ghastly form, giving you a +1 bonus to AC, letting you take unprompted Opportunity Attacks, and can frighten enemies who see you.

This horrific form will gain more power as you level up letting to stop a creatures ability to regain HP, activate Sap or Slow mastery properties even when using a different weapon, and to be able to deal additional damage.

This sub-class is all about starting as a more normal character and as you level up, draw powers from "the strange and ancient horrors of the land," slowly twist your form into becoming your own type of cryptid monster.

I love the idea of this subclass, essentially transforming for a fight, and powering up to become a beast that even a Darklord should be afraid of.

A number of Sub-Classes make the jump to 5.5e

The College of Spirits Bard, Grave Domain Cleric, Phantom Rogue, and Shadow Sorcery Sorcerer (Thought it was known as Shadow Magic), and Undead Patron Warlock are subclasses that are making their D&D 5.5e debut.

As with the previous 'ported' subclasses, with these you'll find that the general gist is the same, but there might be one or two more major changes. Here, I would say mileage is really going to vary, whether you'll want to play one or the other, or consider swapping if you're already using one of these sub-classes in your current campaign.

Each of the Backgrounds present in Ravenloft: The Horrors Within works to inform not only some starting abilities, but also prompts the players for a narrative goal to help in character creation.

For example, the Haunted One explains

"You are haunted by the events of your past. Whether it was a lone terrible incident or an accumulation of painful moments, you bear the unshakable weight of what happened to you. You've tried to bury it and run away from it, to no avail; it can't be slaid with a sword or banished via magic. Nevertheless, you persist.

These new Backgrounds introduced from the release of D&D 5.5e in 2024 continue to impress me. Really paying attention to the narrative lets players work with their DMs to create a character who is far more three-dimensional. A delight to play, and even more fun to craft a story for.

Each of the new Species dives deep into horror and really acts just as you'd expect. The Dhampir has Spider Climb and can bite foes to empower themselves, the Hexblood creates talismans that let them talk and see at a distance, the Lupin's wolf-like abilities let it deal damage with a pounce or howl to unnerve their foes, and the Reborn call upon past lives to increase their chances in the present.

Reading each of the Species got me excited for the possibilities. That being said, the most lackluster of the new Species is the Reborn. Their ability to have Advantage on Death Saving Throws is nice, as is their resistance to gaining Exhaustion in certain scenarios, but those are a fair but more situational than the other species abilities.

With ~40 pages of content highlighting information for the player about the nature of Ravenloft and their character options, we move into the next chapter, where the information for the DM is stored.

Exploring The Domains Of Dread

Chapter 2: Domains of Ravenloft begins the detailed explanation of each of the Domains of Dread. Each section gives you a quick top-down overview of the theme of the Domain, its Darklord, the Genre and Hallmarks of the Domain, Mist Talismans that might allow a player to venture here, and the Aligned and Opposed Tarokka cards.

From this top-down view, we then get to go a little bit deeper, features that a location would be known for, like accursed ruins or vampires, key locations that a player might visit, and then we get a deeper dive into multiple pages of information on the Darklord of a domain, specifically.

These Darklord pages are done with white text on a black background, adding to the imposing status of the Darklord.

In a similar fashion to Eberron: Forge of the Artificer, the book then poses a few levels of depth to assist in the crafting of adventures, too.

In a Campaign Arc, the book suggests the most general of plot threads and recommended foes between the levels of 1-5, 6-8, and 9-10. The game certainly isn't giving you a finished painting, or even a paint-by-numbers, but it is offering you a canvas and some primary colors for you to sit with, think on, and come up with your own masterpiece with your players.

Many chapters also offer adventures for specific levels too. Again, these aren't 'ready to run' in any way, shape, or form, but it is again a structure for an adventure that will let you know what's going on, the plothook and end-goal of the adventure, and even features maps and where monsters should be placed among them.

These are the kinds of adventures that you won't want to pull out at the very last moment, but if you know your table is coming to an off week and you want to surprise your players with something different (or even offer to give your poor forever DM a weekend off) then these are great starting points.

As someone who personally only runs pre-made adventures, a sourcebook like this might not be something I'd normally pick up, outside of wanting more character options for my players, but with the way the book tempts a DM further and further into uncharted territories and playing less guides, it could also be a great gateway to becoming more confident in your own DMing abilities.

As I stated earlier, I've continued to feel that this book is trying to do less about saying "This is horror, or this is why this world is scary" but more of a "You can use these tricks to create horror, and here is a world to use as your playground."

After getting a deep dive into sixteen different Domains of Dread, the book then offers up a smaller blurb of information about two dozen more. For you to take and expand upon your own, or use other source material to expand upon. An example of this might be The Carnival that you can find more information on, including backgrounds to the darklords, in Wild Beyond The Witchlight.

Building Your Own Ravenloft Campaign

These kinds of questions to the DM, posing thought exercises instead of giving one form of answer, continue in the Ravenloft Adventures chapter. When building your own campaign, these questions help you understand that balance between creating a fun adventure for your friends and a horror-filled one.

This section almost feels like a questionnaire for a DM to find the rules that they want to follow. After going through each question, you can learn more about what structure your campaign should fit, which will also help you pace it.

Ravenloft: The Horrors Within provides horror campaign speed dating

Some of these questions include:

  • What horror stories or media inspire your campaign?
  • Do you want your story to explore the unusual nature of the Domain, or is that nature broadly accepted?
  • Can a darklord be defeated, or are they enduring?
  • Does your campaign start with the group together, pulled from different realms, or in media res as something harrowing is taking place?

These questions also advise when you should start to include your characters in the creative process to build a horror adventure together. A lot of these are things that many people have learned to do over the years on their own. It's nice that this baseline "teaching you how to create your own campaign and world" is a core part of the books. Hopefully, DMs pay attention and dive deep into it.

Ravenloft: The Horrors Within Review | Final Thoughts

Ravenloft: The Horrors Within does a fantastic job of inviting the player and DM into the world of Horror. For the player, access to returning and new subclasses and species empowers them to start their own horrific journeys. The DM is provided with ample information about the worlds of Ravenloft, the denizens of the world, and adventure prompts at varying levels of complexity to help ease DMs into testing their mettle.

The real treasure of this collection is the teaching that it imparts. The various questions and prompts to the DM make them not just fill in the blanks, but actually think about different aspects of the world, how they want to approach problems that could be encountered, and how they can involve their players into the world better so it's less about a DM walking players through the world, but the world that the players want to experience getting encountered by their characters.

I'm a big fan of this 'teach by doing' style that has popped up in this book, and a bit by extension in the Eberron book.

Ravenloft: The Horrors Within was reviewed with a copy provided by the publisher



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/reviews/ravenloft-horrors-within-teaches-how-to-add-horror-instead-of-just-what-horror-is
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
